questions.question: Explain the fundamental concept of an interrupt request (IRQ) and describe one primary advantage it provides in a computer system with multiple I/O devices.
questions.modelAnswer: An IRQ is a hardware signal sent by a device to the processor to indicate that it requires immediate attention and has data to transfer or an event to report. It allows the processor to efficiently handle multiple devices without constantly polling each one to check its status, which wastes CPU cycles. This mechanism enables asynchronous operation where the CPU can execute other tasks and only respond to devices when they specifically signal they are ready, improving overall system throughput.

questions.question: Compare and contrast the two main methods for handling I/O: Programmed I/O (Polling) and Interrupt-Driven I/O. Discuss a scenario where one method might be preferred over the other.
questions.modelAnswer: Programmed I/O (Polling) requires the CPU to repeatedly check the status of a device in a loop, consuming significant processing power but offering predictable, simple timing. Interrupt-Driven I/O allows the device to notify the CPU when it is ready, freeing the CPU for other tasks until an interrupt occurs, which is more efficient but introduces latency and overhead for context switching. Polling might be preferred in simple, real-time control systems where predictable response time is critical and the CPU has little else to do, whereas Interrupt-Driven I/O is preferred in general-purpose systems to maximize CPU availability for computation.

questions.question: Describe the process of interrupt handling from the moment a device raises an interrupt request to the moment the CPU returns to its previous task. Your answer should include the role of the interrupt controller, interrupt vector table, and the steps involved in saving and restoring the CPU state.
questions.modelAnswer: When a device raises an interrupt request, the interrupt controller (e.g., 8259 PIC) receives the signal and determines the priority. The CPU finishes its current instruction, acknowledges the interrupt, and the controller provides the interrupt vector number. The CPU uses this number to index into the interrupt vector table, which contains the address of the corresponding ISR. Before jumping to the ISR, the CPU saves its current state (registers, program counter, etc.) onto the stack. The ISR executes to service the device. Upon completion, the CPU restores its saved state from the stack and resumes execution of the interrupted program.
